bull_thesis:
Zealand Pharma is a partner-validated amylin franchise priced as an option on imminent clinical results, sitting on a fortress balance sheet. The thesis rests on five evidence-grounded pillars.

1. The Roche alliance (first quarter 2025) is the strongest external endorsement available to a pre-commercial biotech: co-development, co-commercialization, a 50/50 profit share in the US and EU, plus access to Roche's CT-388 (a dual GLP-1/GIP drug) for a fixed-dose combination. Management held its stated negotiating position through 18 months of public dialogue — 'partners who want to win, not just play' and 'maintain rights in major markets' — and the realized deal matched those terms exactly, demonstrating high promise-to-delivery integrity. Roche carries all manufacturing and commercial capital spending, removing the two existential risks for a pre-revenue biotech: financing and scale-up.

2. The balance sheet is a fortress: net cash of approximately DKK 14.05 billion (as of 31 March 2026), an Altman Z-score (a composite measure of financial health; scores above 3 signal low bankruptcy risk) of 18.4, and trailing twelve-month free cash flow of +DKK 6.32 billion driven by partner inflows. This is real downside cushion against any delay in clinical results and funds the run into Phase III.

3. The amylin mechanism (amylin is a hormone that regulates appetite and slows stomach emptying) has been de-risked by a rival's data: cagrilintide's Phase III (June 2025) showed clean gastrointestinal tolerability and no unexpected safety signals, which management correctly framed as a major de-risking event for the entire drug class. Petrelintide shares the human-amylin backbone and a similar receptor profile, so a clean result for a rival molecule lowers the scientific risk for Zealand's drug.

4. Near-term hard-dated catalysts in the favorable first-half 2026 window: ZUPREME-1, a 42-week Phase II trial of petrelintide (494 patients, average body mass index approximately 37, balanced between men and women — a far more representative population than the 48-patient, roughly 80%-male, low-BMI Phase 1b that management says muted the observed response); and SYNCHRONIZE-1, a 76-week Phase III trial of survodutide in obesity with a cardiovascular-outcomes component. The Phase II readout could confirm the 15–20% weight-loss target with differentiated gastrointestinal tolerability and trigger a meaningful re-rating of the stock.

5. Free second shot: survodutide is fully funded by Boehringer Ingelheim (Phase III plus FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation in metabolic-associated steatohepatitis, or MASH; Phase II showed up to 19% weight loss and fibrosis benefit) — optionality the market may under-credit because it costs Zealand nothing additional. Management also demonstrated owner-like capital discipline by pausing dapiglutide (described internally as its 'most differentiated GLP-1 candidate') on launch-decade return-on-investment logic, and delivered every promised clinical milestone on time, over-delivering on ZUPREME-1 enrollment pace.

Conditions that must hold: ZUPREME-1 must show 15–20% weight loss with the dose-response scaling upward at higher doses (not the approximate 4.8mg-to-9mg flattening seen in men in Phase 1b) and clean gastrointestinal tolerability; survodutide's Phase III must land a competitive efficacy and tolerability profile; and the cash-rich balance sheet must absorb rising commercial-readiness operating costs through Phase III without a dilutive equity raise on adverse terms.
bear_thesis:
This is a pure binary clinical bet mislabeled a value investment, with no margin of safety, two correlated catalysts firing in the same window, and the single most important question dodged by management for a year.

1. The 'value' signal is an artifact. The financials underlying the value label — fiscal 2025 revenue up 14,599%, gross margin 99.7%, net margin 70.0%, earnings per share of DKK 90.22, net income of DKK 6.46 billion — are not operating results. They are the one-time accounting recognition of the Roche upfront payment and milestones, booked into a single fiscal year. The true trajectory is four straight years of net losses (2021: -DKK 1.02 billion; 2022: -DKK 966 million; 2023: -DKK 704 million; 2024: -DKK 1.08 billion), followed by a single deal-driven spike. Consensus forward earnings-per-share growth of -60.5% confirms the market expects that spike to reverse. Underlying operating returns are deeply negative (return on equity -48.3%, return on invested capital -24.9%). Every valuation model on offer — discounted cash flow (DCF, which estimates the present value of future cash flows) fair value of 75.6 versus a price of 326.3, price-to-free-cash-flow of 24.8x, and per-method margin of safety at -100% across DCF, earnings power value, Graham, owner earnings, and intrinsic value at 15% — is non-operative for a pre-commercial biotech and provides no downside anchor. There is no terminal-decline-versus-stable-earnings mispricing to exploit; the price is purely clinical probability multiplied by total addressable market.

2. Binary risk is concentrated, not diversified. Petrelintide's pivotal ZUPREME-1 42-week Phase II and survodutide's SYNCHRONIZE-1 Phase III both read out in the first half of 2026 — and they are not independent: both ride the same obesity, amylin, and incretin-class sentiment and the same regulatory and scientific total-addressable-market narrative. A risk-off turn in obesity-drug sentiment, or a class-wide tolerability scare, hits both at once.

3. The dose-response question is unresolved and has been deflected. Analysts pressed twice (third quarter 2024, second quarter 2025) on curve flattening after approximately weeks 12–13 and the lack of clear separation between 4.8mg and 9mg doses in men. Management's answer was always 'small study; Phase II will resolve it.' Investors are being asked to trust that a 48-patient, predominantly male, low-BMI signal scales to 494 patients at average BMI 37. If ZUPREME-1 repeats the flattening, the 'best-in-class / 15–20% / foundational therapy' thesis is impaired — and management has already moved the goalposts ('end the weight-loss Olympics,' reframing away from peak magnitude toward tolerability) before the data print, a classic expectations-management signal.

4. The run-up is likely already priced in. The Roche deal already re-rated the equity; a positive Phase II is substantially anticipated by an obesity-drug-hungry market, capping upside while a miss would be asymmetric and largely uncushioned (cash protects the company, not the catalyst premium in the stock).

5. Execution stumbles in the easier assets. Glepaglutide was forced into an additional US Phase III; dasiglucagon (for congenital hyperinsulinism, or CHI, a rare condition causing dangerously low blood sugar) is blocked by an un-upgraded third-party manufacturing facility. Both are near-term approvable assets now delayed, a reminder that even Zealand's lower-risk programs slip.

6. Competition and opacity. Eli Lilly (eloralintide) and Novo Nordisk (cagrilintide/CagriSema, plus a stated amylin Phase III) are larger and ahead on commercial muscle. Combination gastrointestinal tolerability effects could compound (Wells Fargo, third quarter 2025). The chief financial officer repeatedly refuses to guide on cash runway ('you don't really guide on the runway anymore') while Zealand's research-and-development cost-share magnitude under the Roche agreement stays undisclosed.

What confirms the bear case: ZUPREME-1 weight loss landing below approximately 15%, or showing the same high-dose flattening at higher body mass index; any gastrointestinal-tolerability disappointment that erases the differentiation thesis; a soft survodutide Phase III; or a dilutive capital raise revealing the runway was tighter than the silence implied.
consensus_delta:
Where we disagree with the market: the street is not pricing terminal decline into stable earnings power, because there is no stable earnings power to misprice. The fiscal 2025 earnings (earnings per share DKK 90.22, net income DKK 6.46 billion, net margin 70%) are a one-time Roche recognition event, and consensus already expects a reversal (forward earnings-per-share growth -60.5%). The genuine expectations question is narrower: has the market correctly priced the probability-weighted outcome of two first-half 2026 binary readouts?

Evidence says the consensus is broadly correct and the easy mispricing is gone. The Roche alliance already re-rated the equity in 2025. Sell-side coverage is dense — Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, Danske Bank, Jefferies, Wells Fargo, UBS, Carnegie, BTIG, and Cantor are all active on the earnings calls. A positive petrelintide Phase II is substantially anticipated by an obesity-drug-hungry market. The only area where the street may be slightly offside is mildly under-crediting survodutide as a free, Boehringer-funded second shot — but that is a thin, two-sided edge, not a clear opportunity.

Critically, the one factually contestable street assumption — that petrelintide's 15–20% target is near-certain — is exactly what management has not proven and has deflected for four consecutive quarters (the 4.8mg-to-9mg dose-response flattening in men). That cuts against a long position: it is an unpriced downside risk, not an unpriced upside. The alpha here lives in the un-read clinical data, not in a market misperception. No exploitable disagreement with consensus exists.
forcing_function:
The catalyst that forces the re-rating is clinical, not corporate. The two events are: (1) ZUPREME-1 42-week Phase II top-line data for petrelintide, guided for the first half of 2026 — the single most important data print. It adjudicates the 15–20% weight-loss target, the durability of the dose-response at average body mass index 37, and the gastrointestinal-tolerability differentiation that the entire 'foundational therapy' thesis rests on. (2) SYNCHRONIZE-1 76-week Phase III top-line results for survodutide in obesity (with a cardiovascular-outcomes component), also first-half 2026, plus additional Phase III obesity readouts across 2026. Phase II initiation of the petrelintide/CT-388 combination (first half 2026) and the Phase IIIa petrelintide monotherapy start (second half 2026) are secondary milestones.

The problem for extracting an edge is that these are hard-binary clinical catalysts — a category where the run-up tends to be priced four to eight weeks ahead of the readout and the result itself resolves a coin-flip on which no outside investor holds an informational advantage. There is no governance reset, proxy window, activist wedge, sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) or spin-off event, distressed or deleveraging driver, or forced-seller deadline — none of the structural, special-situation catalysts that deliver repeatable edge in the current environment. The catalyst is real and dated, but it is the wrong type of catalyst to extract alpha from here.
valley_of_death:
The risky window: near-term (three-to-nine month) risk is concentrated and uncushioned at the equity level. The dominant hazard is the first-half 2026 readout cluster itself. ZUPREME-1 42-week Phase II (petrelintide) and SYNCHRONIZE-1 76-week Phase III (survodutide) print in the same window, and they are correlated — both depend on obesity, amylin, and incretin-class sentiment and the same total-addressable-market narrative. A class-wide tolerability scare or an obesity-drug sentiment turn punishes both simultaneously.

There is no cash-burn hurdle or debt-maturity wall that breaks the company (net cash approximately DKK 14 billion, Altman Z-score 18.4) — the balance sheet protects the enterprise. But the cash does not protect the stock's catalyst premium. With the equity already re-rated on the Roche deal and a positive Phase II largely anticipated, the payoff is asymmetric: a disappointing print (weight loss below approximately 15%, or the high-dose flattening repeating at higher body mass index, or any gastrointestinal-tolerability stumble that erases the differentiation) deflates a premium that the net-cash floor sits far below.

Secondary three-to-nine month risks: dasiglucagon's un-upgraded third-party manufacturing facility (a delayed near-term approvable asset), glepaglutide's extra-Phase III timeline slip, rising commercial-readiness operating costs against a runway the chief financial officer refuses to guide, and the macro tail risk (a potential oil-supply shock and volatility spike) that could reduce appetite for high-beta pre-revenue biotech names regardless of fundamentals. The killer is not a balance-sheet event; it is a binary-data event into a stock that already carries a substantial premium.
positioning_washout:
Forced-selling pressure: limited classic forced-selling dynamics apply here. There is no dividend to cut and no yield-focused shareholder base to flush (pre-revenue biotech; no payout). The shareholder base consists of specialist healthcare and biotech funds plus crossover and growth investors drawn in by the Roche deal and the obesity total-addressable-market narrative, so the mechanical 'income-mandate forces liquidation' thesis does not apply.

The realistic mechanical risk runs the other way: a disappointing ZUPREME-1 print would trigger momentum and crossover capitulation and event-driven unwinds in a name that has already run on the partnership. The current market backdrop — cash levels at sell-signal territory (Bank of America Fund Manager Survey cash at 3.9%), roughly 50% net overweight equities, and limited dry powder to absorb a shock — means a class-sentiment air pocket could cascade through correlated obesity-drug holdings. Note also the dual listing (ZEAL.CO on the Copenhagen exchange plus a US American depositary receipt, or ADR) adds a thin-liquidity, cross-venue wrinkle that can exaggerate price moves around a readout.

Net: there is no clean, pre-dateable washout to buy into. The only mechanical flush on offer is a post-miss one that you would not want to catch without first seeing the data.
moderator_conclusion:
Pass for now, conviction 2 — applying the current investment regime, this is a high-quality company in its worst-suited category, with correlated binary catalysts and no margin of safety.

The forensic analyst scored the business a credible 4 out of 5 (trajectory: turning) and that is fair on company quality: a partner-validated franchise, a fortress net-cash balance sheet (approximately DKK 14 billion, Altman Z-score 18.4), disciplined capital allocation, and on-time clinical delivery. But the moderator's mandate is timing, positioning, and regime fit — and on every one of those axes Zealand fails the current environment.

The current investment regime (baseline 5 June 2026) ranks hard-binary biotech catalysts as the thinnest edge among the available opportunity categories — 'run-up priced four to eight weeks ahead; both agents independently confirmed' — and explicitly deprioritizes this archetype in favor of structural special situations in less-covered categories (distressed or deleveraging situations ahead of spin-offs or forced-seller events). Zealand is none of those. Its forcing function is a pair of hard-binary clinical readouts.

The regime does reward hard-dated catalysts inside the favorable near-term window, and Zealand's first-half 2026 readouts qualify on dating. But the regime's resolution-driver-independence test is decisive against it: petrelintide's Phase II and survodutide's Phase III fire in the same window and share one macro-sentiment driver (obesity-drug class sentiment and total addressable market). The book gets no diversification from holding both — only doubled, correlated exposure, precisely the concentration the regime instructs us to penalize.

Layer on (a) the value label being an artifact of one-time Roche deal recognition (forward earnings-per-share growth -60.5%; all per-method margins of safety at -100%; no valuation anchor), (b) the unresolved, four-quarters-deflected dose-response flattening that sits as an unpriced downside risk into a stock already re-rated on the partnership, and (c) no governance, activist, or sum-of-the-parts wedge to exploit — and the edge collapses. There is no exploitable disagreement with consensus and no clean pre-dateable forced-selling episode to buy into. The alpha lives entirely in un-read coin-flip data where we hold no informational advantage.

The disciplined call is to step aside. This is a name to admire and, at most, revisit after the first-half 2026 Phase II print resolves the dose-response question — either to enter a confirmed franchise on a pullback or to avoid a thesis-impairing miss — but not to size today as either a value or a catalyst position.
interrogator_dossier:
# Forensic Dossier: Zealand Pharma A/S (ZEAL.CO)

Forensic analyst: Claude Opus 4.8. Signal type as filed: 'deep value' (Healthcare). Coverage window: first quarter 2024 through full year 2025 (eight consecutive earnings calls, 16 May 2024 through 19 February 2026).

Threshold caveat the director must absorb first. This company is filed as a value investment, but the transcript record is unambiguous: Zealand is a pre-commercial, partner-funded clinical-stage obesity and metabolic biotech. Product revenue is effectively zero. The screener's 'Revenue +14,599% year-over-year,' 'Gross margin 99.7%,' 'Net margin 70.0%,' 'Earnings per share DKK 90.22,' 'Net income DKK 6.46 billion' for fiscal 2025 are not operating results. They are the accounting echo of the Roche petrelintide licensing deal (upfront payment plus milestone recognition, DKK-denominated) booked into a single fiscal year. Every value ratio derived from those lines — price-to-free-cash-flow 24.8x, free cash flow margin 70.5%, discounted cash flow fair value 75.6 — is therefore an artifact, not a margin-of-safety signal. The genuine thesis is binary clinical catalysts, not earnings power. I assess the narrative on that basis.

## 1. Narrative Arc

The story tightened and de-risked across eight quarters, then narrowed by deliberate portfolio surgery.

First quarter 2024 (May): 'Extremely exciting next few months — everything is live.' Management opened with a broad, optionality-rich pipeline: petrelintide (amylin monotherapy), dapiglutide (a dual GLP-1/GLP-2 agonist), survodutide (partnered to Boehringer Ingelheim for metabolic-associated steatohepatitis and obesity), two rare-disease assets at FDA decision dates in the fourth quarter 2024 (dasiglucagon for congenital hyperinsulinism; glepaglutide for short bowel syndrome), plus early-stage inflammation programs. The tone was 'many irons in the fire, all heating up.' Cash runway had just been extended into 2027 via a January private placement.

Second quarter 2024 (August): The petrelintide readout lands and becomes the central story. Positive Phase 1b 16-week data (4.8%, 8.6%, and 8.3% weight loss across dose cohorts versus 1.7% placebo) triggered a DKK 7 billion equity raise — 'the largest ever European biotech offering.' From here the story visibly re-centers on petrelintide as 'a future backbone therapy,' with the explicit 15–20% weight-loss target for Phase III introduced. Survodutide's metabolic-associated steatohepatitis Phase II (showing fibrosis benefit, earning FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation) became the second pillar.

Third quarter 2024 (November) through full year 2024 (February 2025): Building the organization for a company they do not yet have. ZUPREME-1 Phase IIb initiated (December 2024), 494 patients, five dose arms up to 9mg. Commercial (Eric Cox, chief commercial officer) and scientific (Utpal Singh, chief scientific officer, formerly 18 years at Eli Lilly) hires signal a deliberate shift from 'get the data' to 'be ready to commercialize.' Glepaglutide hits a regulatory wall: management concedes 'we are quite certain that we need to do' an additional Phase III in the US — a delay, disclosed promptly and calmly.

First quarter 2025 (May): The franchise-maker — the Roche alliance. 'Zealand has never been in a stronger position than we are today.' The Roche petrelintide deal — co-development, co-commercialization, 50/50 profit share in the US and Europe, plus access to Roche's CT-388 (a GLP-1/GIP dual agonist) for a fixed-dose combination — is the single most consequential event in the coverage window. It removes the two existential biotech risks: financing and manufacturing and commercial build (Roche owns the capital spending for supply). The narrative pivots from 'promising molecule' to 'leading amylin franchise.'

Second quarter 2025 (August) through third quarter 2025 (November): Confidence hardens; the portfolio is pruned. Cagrilintide Phase III data (Novo Nordisk) is repeatedly invoked as a 'major de-risking event' for the amylin drug class. Then the disciplined cut: dapiglutide is paused in the third quarter 2025 — the 'most differentiated GLP-1-containing mid-stage candidate' set aside because the GLP-1 lane is 'increasingly crowded' and the differentiation bar for a 2030s commercial launch is 'long, complex, and expensive.' Dasiglucagon (for congenital hyperinsulinism) hits a third-party manufacturing classification issue; a contingency supplier is qualified.

Full year 2025 (February 2026): 'The most defining and catalyst-rich year in Zealand's history.' Two hard binary readouts are set up for the first half of 2026: ZUPREME-1 42-week Phase II (petrelintide) and SYNCHRONIZE-1 76-week Phase III (survodutide in obesity, with a cardiovascular-outcomes component). A GIP analog program is restarted; a Boston high-throughput and artificial-intelligence research hub is announced alongside a '10 candidates into the clinic in 5 years / 5 launches in 5 years' generational-biotech ambition.

Net arc: broad and hopeful, to single-asset-validated, to partner-de-risked, to portfolio-disciplined, to catalyst-gated. Growth drivers added: Roche alliance, CT-388 combination, survodutide cardiovascular-outcomes trial, GIP program restart, discovery platform. Growth drivers removed: dapiglutide (paused), early-stage inflammation programs (de-emphasized). The thesis is now almost entirely: 'Does petrelintide's Phase II confirm 15–20% with clean gastrointestinal tolerability, and does survodutide's Phase III deliver?'

## 2. Claims vs. Financials (Forensic)

The central forensic finding: the financials provided are a deal-distorted snapshot that does not describe an operating business. The task is to separate signal (cash runway, partner economics) from noise (the one-year revenue and earnings spike).

Management claim: 'Strong — and strongest-ever — financial position; funded toward profitability.' What the calls said: cash approximately DKK 9 billion at the start of 2025; 'can easily fund Phase II'; Roche covers manufacturing. What the financials show: net cash approximately -DKK 14.05 billion (net cash, live 31 March 2026 correction); net debt -DKK 2.12 billion (screener field); Altman Z-score 18.4 (a solvency fortress); trailing twelve-month free cash flow +DKK 6.32 billion. Assessment: corroborated, but for the wrong reason. The cash and 'positive free cash flow' are partnership inflows (Roche upfront and milestones), not operations. Balance-sheet strength is real and is the load-bearing fact; the 'earnings' are not recurring.

Management claim: 'Transformational growth.' What the calls said: fiscal 2025 revenue DKK 9.21 billion, net income DKK 6.46 billion, earnings per share DKK 90.22, versus prior-year losses. What the financials show: multi-year line — 2021 net income -DKK 1.02 billion, 2022 -DKK 966 million, 2023 -DKK 704 million, 2024 -DKK 1.08 billion, 2025 +DKK 6.46 billion. Assessment: misleading if read as operating results. Four straight years of roughly DKK 700 million to DKK 1.08 billion in net losses, then a single +DKK 6.46 billion year that exactly coincides with the Roche deal. This is a one-off recognition event, not an inflection in commercial earnings. Forward consensus earnings-per-share growth of -60.5% confirms the market expects the spike to reverse.

Management claim: 'Best-in-class petrelintide; 15–20% weight loss.' What the calls said: every call since second quarter 2024; reaffirmed third quarter 2025 and full year 2025. What the financials show: no commercial revenue; return on equity -48.3%; return on invested capital -24.9% (true underlying returns on the operating base are deeply negative). Assessment: unverifiable and forward-looking. The efficacy claim rests on a 48-patient Phase 1b trial (predominantly male, low body mass index) — management itself flags these as response-muting characteristics. The pivotal 494-patient ZUPREME-1 42-week data has not read out as of the last earnings call. The claim is a hypothesis, not a result.

Management claim: 'Amylin class de-risked by cagrilintide Phase III.' Assessment: partially valid. True that a competitor's long-acting amylin showing clean gastrointestinal tolerability de-risks the drug mechanism. It does not de-risk petrelintide's molecule-specific dose-response, which only ZUPREME-1 can answer. Management is borrowing a rival's data to backstop its own un-read trial — legitimate but not a substitute.

Management claim: 'Disciplined capital allocation' (dapiglutide pause). Assessment: corroborated and credible. Setting aside a sunk-cost asset because the launch-decade differentiation bar is too high is exactly the discipline you want from a cash-rich pre-revenue biotech. Genuine positive signal.

Management claim: Earnings-beat rate 57% (4 of 7 quarters). Assessment: not meaningful. Earnings-beat rate has no informational content for a pre-revenue biotech whose earnings per share is dominated by deal timing and non-cash items. Do not weight it.

Bottom line for this section: the only financial facts that survive scrutiny are (a) a fortress balance sheet and net-cash position funded by partners, and (b) deeply negative underlying operating returns masked by one-time deal income. The valuation models (discounted cash flow fair value 75.6, price-to-free-cash-flow, all per-method margins of safety at -100%) are non-operative for a binary-catalyst biotech and should be discarded rather than interpreted as a margin-of-safety signal.

## 3. Tone and Confidence Trajectory: Rising, Then Plateauing at High Confidence

First quarter 2024: 'Extremely exciting next few months… key clinical results from all our programs.' Read: optimistic, broad, event-anticipatory.

Second quarter 2024: 'Largest ever European biotech offering… transformational potential.' Read: confidence plus capital validation.

Third quarter 2024: 'Consistently demonstrated best-in-class potential.' Read: first use of 'best-in-class' as settled fact.

Full year 2024: 'Transformational year… strongest possible foundation.' Read: self-congratulatory, forward-leaning.

First quarter 2025: 'Zealand has never been in a stronger position than we are today.' Read: peak conviction — the Roche-deal high.

Second quarter 2025: 'Exceedingly well positioned… major de-risking event.' Read: sustained high confidence.

Third quarter 2025: 'Strong execution and continued momentum… catalyst-rich chapter.' Read: confident and disciplined (the dapiglutide pause framed as strength).

Full year 2025: 'The most defining and catalyst-rich year in Zealand's history.' Read: confidence intact, now explicitly betting on first-half 2026 readouts.

Direction: clearly rising from first quarter 2024 through first quarter 2025, then stable-high through full year 2025. Crucially, the language stayed offensive — growth, investment, franchise-building — throughout. There is no defensive pivot to cost-cutting, efficiency, or macro-survival, which is the tell you watch for in a deteriorating biotech. Guidance specificity on clinical timelines stayed high and consistent. The one area where specificity was deliberately withheld is financial runway: the chief financial officer, first quarter 2025 — 'you don't really guide on the runway anymore' — a notable and repeated non-answer.

## 4. Guidance Credibility: Medium-High on Clinical and Operational; Low-Information on Financial

This is a biotech, so 'guidance' means clinical timelines and trial-execution promises, not revenue forecasts. On that axis the track record is good.

Petrelintide Phase 1b (promised second quarter 2024): delivered on time, positive results, second quarter 2024. Confirmed.

DKK 7 billion equity raise (capability claimed): executed, oversubscribed, second quarter 2024. Confirmed.

ZUPREME-1 initiation (guided second half 2024): first patient dosed December 2024; enrollment completed ahead of schedule — 'significantly faster than initially anticipated.' Genuine over-delivery on enrollment pace. Confirmed with upside.

Roche partnership (telegraphed as 'initiating dialogues' second quarter 2024): signed first quarter 2025, on the strategic terms management had publicly insisted on (50/50 profit share, retained commercial rights in major markets). Promise-to-delivery integrity is high. Confirmed.

28-week ZUPREME-1 primary-endpoint visit (guided): completed late September 2025, on track. Confirmed.

Misses and slips: glepaglutide — management implied a clean US regulatory path early, then conceded by full year 2024 that a full additional Phase III is required (a material timeline slip, disclosed promptly and calmly). Dasiglucagon (congenital hyperinsulinism) — FDA-decision-date-era optimism from first quarter 2024 had not resolved by third quarter 2025, where an un-upgraded third-party manufacturing facility is forcing a contingency-supplier qualification. A real, unresolved operational drag on a near-term approvable asset. Dapiglutide — progressed confidently for six quarters, then paused. Defensible on the merits, but anyone who underwrote dapiglutide optionality took a write-down.

Sandbagging? No evidence of lowballing. If anything management is aspirationally high (the '5 launches in 5 years / 10 clinical candidates in 5 years' framing at full year 2025 is a stretch target, not a sandbag). Reliability: medium-high on what they control (trial execution, deal-making), with a caveat for two rare-disease execution stumbles and a financial-runway information vacuum.

## 5. Analyst Pressure Points

Analysts circled the same few soft spots repeatedly.

The weight-loss curve flattening at high dose and the 4.8mg-to-9mg non-separation. Thomas Bowers (Danske Bank, third quarter 2024) and Carsten Lønborg Madsen (Danske Bank, second quarter 2025) both pressed on the lack of clear additional response in men when doubling from 4.8 to 9mg, and on the curve appearing to flatten after approximately weeks 12–13. Management's answer was consistently: 'small study, low-BMI male population, individual variability — Phase II will resolve it.' This is the single most important open question, and it is precisely what ZUPREME-1's 42-week readout will adjudicate. Management is asking investors to trust that a 48-patient signal scales to 494 patients at higher body mass index. If the Phase II shows the same dose-response flattening, the 'best-in-class / 15–20% / foundational therapy' thesis is impaired.

Financial runway. Multiple analysts (Laura Hindley, Morgan Stanley, among others) asked what the cash position supports through Phase III. The chief financial officer repeatedly declined to answer: 'you don't really guide on the runway anymore.' For a company whose entire thesis depends on funding through expensive Phase III trials and a pre-launch commercial build, the refusal to frame runway is a persistent, deliberate opacity.

Combination-therapy gastrointestinal tolerability compounding. Mohit Bansal (Wells Fargo, third quarter 2025) pushed the sharpest scientific challenge: amylin itself has gastrointestinal effects; stacking it with a GLP-1/GIP drug (CT-388) could compound tolerability problems — undercutting the entire 'better patient experience' differentiator. Management's 'we maximize the amylin and add only a teaspoon of GLP-1' answer is a hypothesis, untested in humans for this specific combination (Phase II starts only in the first half of 2026).

Partner economics and cost-share caps. Ollie Burrow (Goldman Sachs, first quarter 2025) probed whether Zealand has uncapped research-and-development cost exposure under the Roche 50/50 agreement. Management confirmed it shares research-and-development costs (Roche carries manufacturing capital spending) but declined to quantify Zealand's spending obligation — leaving the magnitude genuinely unclear.

Competitive encroachment in amylin (Eli Lilly's eloralintide, Novo Nordisk's cagrilintide/CagriSema, AbbVie/Boehringer Ingelheim). Asked every call. Management's posture shifted from 'we're unique' to 'we welcome competition; it validates the drug class' — a reasonable but convenient reframe.

Pattern: the deflected or under-answered topics — runway, cost-share magnitude, dose-response durability — are all the ones that would matter most in a downside scenario. None has yet turned into bad news except glepaglutide (foreshadowed) and dasiglucagon manufacturing.

## 6. Red Flags and Green Flags

Positive factors: (1) The Roche alliance on retained-rights terms (first quarter 2025). A top-tier pharmaceutical company validating petrelintide with 50/50 economics and taking manufacturing and commercial capital-spending risk is the most credible external endorsement available to a pre-commercial biotech. It removes financing and scale-up as existential risks. (2) Capital discipline (dapiglutide pause, third quarter 2025). Setting aside the 'most differentiated' but sub-threshold asset on launch-decade return-on-investment logic is the behavior of management allocating capital like owners, not empire-builders. (3) Over-delivery on ZUPREME-1 enrollment pace and consistent on-time delivery of every promised clinical milestone in the coverage window. (4) Class-level de-risking from cagrilintide Phase III (second quarter 2025) genuinely lowers the mechanism risk for amylin as a drug class. (5) Fortress balance sheet and net-cash position (Altman Z-score 18.4; net cash approximately DKK 14 billion) — real downside cushion against catalyst slippage. (6) Survodutide as a second, partner-funded shot at the target (Boehringer Ingelheim-funded Phase III plus Breakthrough Therapy designation in metabolic-associated steatohepatitis) — optionality the market may under-credit, at zero incremental cost to Zealand.

Negative factors: (1) The entire equity thesis is binary and un-read. Petrelintide's pivotal efficacy (ZUPREME-1 42-week) and survodutide's Phase III (SYNCHRONIZE-1) both read out in the same first-half 2026 window — concentration of binary risk, not diversification. (2) Unresolved dose-response question (4.8mg approximately equals 9mg in men; curve flattening) that management has deflected for four quarters. (3) Two rare-disease execution stumbles: glepaglutide forced into an extra Phase III; dasiglucagon blocked by an un-upgraded third-party manufacturing facility — both are near-term approvable assets now delayed. (4) Runway opacity: the chief financial officer will not guide it; cost-share magnitude is undisclosed. (5) Financials are deal-distorted — any screening tool that reads the fiscal 2025 spike as recurring earnings is being misled by the data. (6) Valuation has no margin-of-safety anchor: all per-method margins of safety at -100%, no commercial cash flows to discount; the price is a pure function of clinical probability multiplied by total addressable market.

## 7. Hidden Signals

From 'alternative to GLP-1' to 'foundational first-choice therapy.' The terminology escalated deliberately. Early calls positioned petrelintide humbly as an alternative for patients who cannot tolerate GLP-1 drugs; by third quarter 2025 and full year 2025 it is described as 'the foundational and first-choice therapy' for the vast majority. Same drug, materially larger implied total addressable market. Watch whether the Phase II data earns that escalation or whether it is narrative inflation ahead of the print.

'End the weight-loss Olympics.' A repeated rhetorical move (third quarter 2025, full year 2025): reframing away from peak weight-loss magnitude — where Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk have the advantage — toward tolerability and persistence, where amylin can win. This is expectations management — pre-emptively redefining the scoreboard so that a 15–20% result (not 25%+) reads as a win rather than a miss. Astute, but investors should note the goalposts were moved before the data.

Competition reframe: 'We welcome more competition… it validates the category' (first quarter 2025). Classic conversion of a threat (Eli Lilly's eloralintide, Novo Nordisk's amylin program) into a tailwind. Partly true (class validation), partly defensive.

Management bench deepened toward commercialization and discovery: chief commercial officer Eric Cox (third quarter 2024), chief scientific officer Utpal Singh, formerly 18 years at Eli Lilly (first quarter 2025), chief development officer Steven Johnson (second quarter 2025). The hiring pattern signals genuine belief in a multi-asset, durable company — but also rising fixed-cost operating expenses that the un-guided runway must absorb.

The 'generational biotech / 5 launches in 5 years / Boston artificial-intelligence hub' theme (full year 2025) is a new, larger story layered on top just as the make-or-break Phase II approaches — a possible signal that management wants the market focused on a long-horizon platform narrative regardless of any single readout. Could reflect conviction; could be a hedge against binary risk.

GIP analog restarted (full year 2025) right after dapiglutide was paused. The portfolio is being actively reshaped around the Roche and CT-388 combination axis (amylin backbone plus incretin add-ons), confirming the strategic center of gravity has moved decisively to the amylin franchise.

## 8. Capital-Allocation Verdict for the Director: Strengthening, Binary-Gated

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory turning; quality improving. Over eight quarters Zealand converted a hopeful multi-asset pipeline into a partner-validated, well-capitalized, disciplined amylin franchise with a credible second shot (survodutide) carried at a partner's cost. Management has, on the things it controls — deal-making, trial execution, capital discipline — done what it said it would do, with high promise-to-delivery integrity. The balance sheet is a fortress. The strategic narrative is coherent and improving for the right reasons.

But 'strengthening' describes the setup, not a realized value. This is not a deep-value situation in any conventional sense. There is no discounted stream of stable earnings; the fiscal 2025 earnings are a one-time Roche recognition event, and every valuation model on offer (discounted cash flow 75.6, price-to-free-cash-flow, margins of safety at -100%) is non-operative for a pre-commercial binary-catalyst biotech. The equity is a call option on first-half 2026 clinical data, cushioned by net cash.

The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing a position: both pivotal de-risking events — petrelintide ZUPREME-1 42-week Phase II and survodutide SYNCHRONIZE-1 Phase III — print in the same first-half 2026 window, and the one open question management has dodged for a year (does petrelintide's dose-response hold and scale, or does the 4.8mg-to-9mg flattening repeat at higher body mass index?) is exactly what that readout adjudicates. Position size should be set as a binary-event option — define the downside on a disappointing print first, size to survive it — not as a value or margin-of-safety position, because there is no margin of safety here except the cash, and the cash does not protect the equity's catalyst premium.
